senses.definition: able to exist in two stable states or configurations, typically switching between them under certain conditions
examples: The bistable switch can be flipped between on and off states by a small input signal.
Bi-stable devices are used in some memory circuits.

senses.definition: plural of bistable; devices or systems that have two stable states
examples: The circuit contains several bistables that store binary information.
Researchers study bistables in neural and electronic contexts.

senses.definition: involving or relating to two states; having two distinct states or conditions
examples: A bistate system can switch between two configurations.
The country is a bistate federation with two capitals.

senses.definition: relating to or denoting a double crown of thorns; used in religious or heraldic contexts
examples: Bistephanic symbolism appears in certain medieval manuscripts.
The coat of arms features a bisthepanic motif.

senses.definition: a brownish-black or yellowish-black pigment derived from burnt wood or fossil resin; a color
examples: The painter mixed the bister pigment for the shadows.
Her dress had tones of bister and ochre.

senses.definition: having been covered with or rendered in bister (a dark pigment); stained dark
examples: The wood was bistered to deepen the shadow tones.
The canvas was bistered to create a moody atmosphere.

senses.definition: plural of bister; brownish-black pigments or stains
examples: The artist used several bisters to create depth.
Different botanic resins yield various bisters.

senses.definition: a chemical compound containing four tetrazole rings; used in energetic materials or research contexts
examples: Bistetrazole derivatives are studied for their explosive properties.
The lab synthesized a new bistetrazole compound.

senses.definition: a poetical or archaic plural of 'bist' or a mistaken/rare term; in some contexts refers to a type of belt or ornament; may be dialectal
examples: Antique texts mention bisti in wearables.
The glossary notes bisti as an obsolete term.

senses.definition: having two pointed projections or peduncles; anatomically double-pronged
examples: The bistipular leaf segments resemble two tiny projections.
A bistipular structure was observed in the specimen.

senses.definition: to cause to have two pointed projections or to form two prongs
examples: The plant may bistipulate as it matures.
A bistipulate blade divided into two tips.

senses.definition: having two prongs or points; two-pointed
examples: A bistipuled beak helps in cracking seeds.
The leaf is bistipuled at its tip.

senses.definition: a perennial plant of the genus Persicaria with pink or white flowers; also refers to the root used in herbal medicine; common name for certain species
examples: Bistort is grown for its decorative spikes.
Herbalists sometimes use bistort root for remedies.

senses.definition: to twist or bend sharply; to distort
examples: The path bistorted as the river cut through.
Her stance bistorted under pressure.

senses.definition: the plant genus Persicaria bistorta, commonly called bistort; used in ornamental gardening
examples: Persicaria bistorta is known for its dense flower spikes.
Bistorta can thrive in damp soils.

senses.definition: plural of bistort; plants of the Persicaria bistorta group or the flower spikes
examples: Gardens featured several bistorts in late spring.
The stems supported clusters of bistorts.

senses.definition: plural of bistoury; a surgical scalpel with a curved blade; a term for surgical instruments in old texts
examples: The antique set included several bistouries.
The surgeon used a bistoury for precision cuts.

senses.definition: an action in sculpture or carving involving turning or twisting shapes; in film, a technique of montage; in art, a type of cut or turning movement
examples: The artist studied bistournage to add dynamic form.
Cinematography sometimes uses bistournage for transitions.

senses.definition: a decorative edge or molding with a double bend or twist; a sculptural feature
examples: The frame features bistournoy embellishments.
Architects used bistournoy to create a doubled profile.

senses.definition: a surgical knife; a bistoury; a curved blade used in delicate operations
examples: The surgeon kept a small bistoury ready.
Historical texts describe the use of bistoury in dissections.

senses.definition: having two distinct layers or levels, especially in geology or anatomy
examples: The rock showed a bistratal layering pattern.
Bistratal structures were observed under the microscope.

senses.definition: divided into two stratified layers; occurring in two layers
examples: A bistratose pattern appeared in the sedimentary rock.
The tissue is bistratose, with two distinct cell layers.

senses.definition: a brownish-black pigment or the color produced by it; also a dishwater-gray brown color
examples: The artist used bistre for the shadows.
The old document was written in a faint bistre ink.

senses.definition: stained or colored with bistre
examples: The manuscript pages were bistred with age.
Bistred edges added a vintage feel.

senses.definition: plural of bistre; brownish-black pigments or inks
examples: The palette contained several bistres.
Artists mixed bistres to shade the scene.

senses.definition: to stripe or mark with two distinct lines or bands
examples: The fabric was bistriated with two bold lines.
The wings were bistriated in black and white.

senses.definition: a chemical compound containing two triazole rings; used in research or pharmaceutical contexts
examples: Bistriazole derivatives show potential in drug design.
The synthesis focused on a bistrazole core.
